one_line_takeaway: AlphaAlign shows that a lightweight RLVR setup with only binary harmful/benign prompt labels and a dual verifiable-safety/normalized-helpfulness reward can, within under 200 PPO steps, drive large ASR reductions across StrongREJECT, AdvBench, WildGuardTest, JailbreakTrigger, PAIR, and GCG while simultaneously raising CoCoNot over-refusal accuracy, outperforming Direct Refusal, Circuit Breaker, and SCoT baselines on 3B-7B Qwen/Llama backbones.
key_finding: On Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct + AlphaAlign vs. the unmodified Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct and the three baselines (Table 2): AlphaAlign reduces StrongREJECT ASR from 3.51% to 0.31%, AdvBench ASR from 0.96% to 0.0%, WildGuardTest ASR from 31.6% to 6.38%, JailbreakTrigger ASR from 27.6% to 3.75%, PAIR ASR from 67.69% to 4.61%, and GCG ASR from 49.04% to 0.77%, while raising CoCoNot (over-refusal) accuracy from 88.92% to 91.29% -- the best score among all compared methods on every column in that row, whereas Direct Refusal, Circuit Breaker, and SCoT each underperform on at least one axis (e.g., Direct Refusal is weak on PAIR/GCG; Circuit Breaker is weak on WildGuardTest/JailbreakTrigger; SCoT drops CoCoNot accuracy to 74.93% on the 3B model). Similar patterns hold for Llama3.2-3B-Instruct and Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct backbones (e.g., Qwen2.5-7B + AlphaAlign: StrongREJECT and AdvBench both 0.0%, PAIR 0.19%, GCG 0.0%, CoCoNot 93.14%). On utility (Table 3), AlphaAlign changes MMLU by -0.1/-2.1/-1.6 points across the three instruct backbones respectively, while AlpacaEval rises by +6.7/+10.0/+7.9 points and GSM8K changes by +4.4/-8.3/+2.9 points depending on backbone (Llama3.2-3B-Instruct shows a GSM8K drop of 8.3 points). AlphaAlign-Zero (verifiable safety reward only, applied to the base Qwen2.5-3B model with no instruction tuning) shows large safety gains over Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct's conventional post-training on the reported jailbreak/utility figure, and training-dynamics tracking shows ASR dropping and format-compliance rising within under 200 RL steps while over-refusal rate decreases over training. The ablation (removing the helpfulness reward) shows utility and CoCoNot scores degrade relative to full AlphaAlign, supporting the claim that the helpfulness term is necessary to avoid trading utility for safety. The CKAS analysis shows the Direct-Refusal instruct model has high cumulative adoption of jailbreak-inducing tokens (e.g., "here", "is") and low adoption of safety-critical tokens ("illegal", "harm", "unethical", "dangerous") early in generation, whereas AlphaAlign shows the reverse pattern, offered as evidence of "deep" rather than shallow/pattern-matched refusal.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is AlphaAlign, a pure reinforcement-learning-with-verifiable-rewards (RLVR) framework for LLM safety alignment that uses a dual-component reward: (1) a verifiable safety reward that checks a structured output (a <safety_reasoning> block followed by an <answer> block) against a binary ground-truth harmfulness label of the prompt, rewarding correctly-formatted, justified refusals of harmful queries and penalizing refusals of benign ones; and (2) a normalized (group-relative, GRPO-style) helpfulness reward applied only to non-refusal responses to benign prompts that exceed the batch-mean helpfulness score. The model is trained with PPO under this reward using only binary prompt-level safety labels (no supervised chain-of-thought safety data, no handcrafted safety specifications). A variant, AlphaAlign-Zero, applies only the verifiable safety reward to a base (non-instruction-tuned) model to study safety self-evolution in isolation.
threat_model: The paper addresses adversarial misuse of LLM outputs: static harmful-content requests (StrongREJECT, AdvBench), static jailbreak prompt sets (WildGuardTest, JailbreakTrigger), and adaptive/optimization-based jailbreak attacks (PAIR, GCG) generated against AdvBench harmful questions. The defender goal is to refuse genuinely harmful requests and resist these jailbreak attacks while avoiding over-refusal of benign/contrastive-safe prompts (measured via CoCoNot) and without degrading general utility (MMLU, AlpacaEval, GSM8K). Attack success is scored by attack success rate (ASR), first via the paper's own refusal verifier, then cross-checked with Llama-Guard-3-8B (paper text says "Llama3-Guard-8B").
limitations: The authors' own stated limitations (Section 5): (1) the safety verifier relies solely on a binary safety label and a simple string-matching refusal verifier against a small set of known refusal phrases, leaving more sophisticated rule-based or learned verifiers unexplored; (2) although training converges in under 200 RL steps on the tested jailbreak sets, the paper does not test more complex or dynamically adapting jailbreak datasets during RL training; (3) due to compute constraints, AlphaAlign was not evaluated on larger models than 7B. Additional caveats evident from the text itself: the refusal verifier used for both training reward and part of ASR evaluation is pattern-based, which could be gamed by attacks that elicit harmful content without triggering the specific refusal-phrase patterns it checks for, and the same verifier partially underlies the reported ASR numbers (cross-checked with Llama-Guard, but the paper does not report reward-hacking analyses or verifier-attack robustness beyond that). Utility trade-offs are backbone-dependent and not uniformly positive (e.g., the 8.3-point GSM8K drop for Llama3.2-3B-Instruct), which the paper reports but does not deeply analyze.

quantitative_results: Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct + AlphaAlign: StrongREJECT 0.0%, AdvBench 0.0%, PAIR 0.19%, GCG 0.0%, CoCoNot 93.14%
Llama3.2-3B-Instruct + AlphaAlign: WildGuardTest 2.43%, JailbreakTrigger 1.5%, PAIR 0.57%, GCG 0.76%, CoCoNot 91.29%
RL convergence in fewer than 200 training steps yields substantial safety gains (paper's stated efficiency claim)
baselines_compared: Circuit Breaker (Zou et al. 2024, representation-engineering interruption)
SCoT (Yang et al. 2025, distillation-based reasoning safety alignment)
